title: St. Solomon the King — St. Agrippina of Rome & St. Aristocleus the Martyr
narrative:
title": "St. Solomon the King — St. Agrippina of Rome & St. Aristocleus the Martyr

narrative": "title\": \"St. Solomon the King — St. Agrippina of Rome & St. Aristocleus the Martyr

narrative\": \"St. Solomon the King and Prophet of Israel, St. Agrippina of Rome the Virgin-Martyr, and St. Aristocleus the Presbyter-Martyr of Cyprus

On the twenty-third of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the feast of the holy Prophet and King Solomon, the son of David and Bathsheba, the builder of the Temple in Jerusalem, the author in the tradition of Proverbs, the Song of Songs, and Ecclesiastes, the man whose wisdom became the standard against which all subsequent wisdom was measured and whose name became synonymous with the divine gift of understanding. Solomon's place in the sanctoral calendar of the Ethiopian church is particularly honoured: the Solomonic dynasty of Ethiopia traced its descent from the union of Solomon and the Queen of Sheba — the Queen of the South who came to hear Solomon's wisdom, as Jesus recalls in the Gospel — and the tradition of the Ethiopian church regards Solomon not merely as a Hebrew patriarch but as an ancestor of the royal house from which the emperors of Ethiopia claimed their legitimacy, making him a figure of national as well as religious significance.

The theology of Solomon in the Tewahedo tradition is a theology of wisdom as gift and as responsibility: Solomon asked God not for long life or riches or the death of his enemies but for wisdom — for an understanding heart that could discern between good and evil, could govern the people rightly, could perceive the truth in contested matters — and God gave him what he asked and more. The tradition honours Solomon for the act of asking as much as for the wisdom he received: the king who knew what to ask for was already demonstrating the beginning of wisdom, which is the knowledge of what one lacks and the willingness to seek it from the right source. But the tradition does not sentimentalise Solomon: it acknowledges that wisdom given can also be squandered, that the builder of the Temple also multiplied wives who turned his heart toward foreign gods, and that the brilliant reign that began in the gift of wisdom ended in a kingdom about to be divided. Solomon is the tradition's icon of the tragic possibilities of the gifted life, as well as the glorious ones.

Saint Agrippina of Rome is a virgin-martyr of the early persecutions whose feast is celebrated across both the Eastern and Western traditions: a young Roman woman of noble family who was condemned for the profession of her faith, who underwent torture before her death, and whose body was miraculously transported after her death from Rome to Sicily — the tradition records that three women named Bassa, Paula, and Agathonike received in a dream divine instructions to take her body to Minas in Sicily — where it became the centre of a great cult and the source of numerous miracles. Agrippina is the tradition's icon of the mobile martyr: the witness whose body does not stay where her death occurred but is carried, by divine instruction, to the place where her intercession will be most needed and most fruitful. Her translation from Rome to Sicily is the tradition's earliest and most literal version of the theology of the relics: the martyr goes where God sends her, and the community that receives her body receives with it the intercession of one who has already completed the journey that they are still making.

Saint Aristocleus the Presbyter-Martyr of Cyprus completes the assembly of this feast as one of the witnesses of the Cypriot tradition — the island church that has given the greater calendar so many saints, from Barnabas who died there to Spyridon who lived and worked there to Lazarus who was its first bishop. Aristocleus was a priest martyred for the faith, his presbyteral office making his death a particularly specific attack on the sacramental life of the Church: to kill the priest is to attempt to kill the community's access to the Eucharist, to cut off the priestly mediation through which the sacrifice of Christ is made present at the altar. The tradition's insistence on preserving the names of martyred presbyters alongside the martyred bishops is a statement about the ecclesial body as a whole: not only the leaders but the entire sacramental structure of the Church was the target of persecution, and not only the leaders but the entire structure held firm. The twenty-third day of Sene gathers the wisest of Israel's kings alongside a Roman noble-woman whose body God moved across the sea and a Cypriot priest who kept the altar until the altar cost him everything. Amen.
